summary:
The Minoan eruption of Thera (Santorini) was one of the largest volcanic events in human history, a catastrophic explosive eruption that ejected an estimated 60 cubic kilometers of material, generated massive tsunamis, and deposited volcanic ash across the eastern Mediterranean. The precise date is debated — radiocarbon dating suggests approximately 1620–1600 BC, while archaeological and ice-core evidence allows for dates ranging into the late seventeenth century BC.

The eruption destroyed the thriving [[event:53|Minoan]]-influenced settlement of Akrotiri on Thera, which had been a sophisticated urban community with multi-story buildings, elaborate drainage systems, and magnificent wall paintings depicting nature, festivals, and sea voyages. The inhabitants apparently evacuated before the final eruption — no bodies have been found, and valuables were removed — but the city was buried under volcanic ash and perfectly preserved, giving it the epithet the Pompeii of the Aegean.

The broader effects on Minoan civilization remain debated. The eruption produced tsunamis that may have devastated Minoan coastal towns on Crete and disrupted agricultural systems through ash fall. Some scholars have linked the eruption to the subsequent decline of Minoan civilization and eventually to its replacement by [[event:57|Mycenaean]] Greek control around 1450 BC, though others argue the chronological gap between the eruption and the Minoan collapse is too large for a direct causal link. The Thera eruption has also been proposed — speculatively — as a possible inspiration for the Atlantis legend recorded by Plato.
